monomer-enhanced activity: The mouse L1 5'UTR consists of tandem monomeric repeats, wherein the second monomer consistently enhances overall promoter activity

monocistronic ORF2 mRNA expression: Expression pattern of LINE-1 transcripts in the human brain that exclusively encode the ORF2 protein

cleavage abnormality: Phenomena during embryonic cell division including accelerated or asymmetric cleavage, cleavage arrest, and degeneration
derepression: Relief of the repressed state of gene or transposon expression, resulting in increased activity

trans-retrotransposition: LINE-1 mediates the retrotransposition of other sequences, including Alu, SVA, and mRNA

reduced promoter CpG methylation: Decreased methylation at CpG sites within the LINE-1 promoter region, typically associated with transcriptional activation

promoter methylation: An epigenetic modification that typically suppresses the activity of genes or transposable elements
promoter binding: The binding of specific proteins or protein complexes to the L1Hs promoter region to regulate its transcription

transposition in differentiated primary: LINE-1 retrotransposition occurring in differentiated primary human somatic cells

transposition in non-dividing somatic ce: Retrotransposition of LINE-1 occurring in post-mitotic, non-dividing somatic cells
gene/exon/regulatory element translocati: 3' transduction mediates the relocation of genes, exons, and regulatory elements to novel genomic sites
genotypic variation: Genomic sequence heterogeneity existing between distinct cells within a single individual
gene duplication: Replication of genes or gene fragments to new genomic loci via retrotransposon-mediated sequence transduction

Post-Translational Regulation: A mechanism regulating retrotransposition by modulating the nuclear accessibility of L1 ribonucleoprotein complexes
Enhanced Translation Efficiency: eIF3 binding to m6A-modified sites on L1 RNA enhances the translation efficiency of L1 proteins
Translational Regulation: The process controlling protein synthesis rates, utilized to restrict the production of retrotransposon proteins
Aggregation: The polymerization of human L1 ORF1 protein under low-salt conditions
Intratumoral Heterogeneity: Genotypic or phenotypic differences among cancer cells within the same tumor, associated with therapeutic resistance and poor prognosis
Tumor Genome Plasticity: The capacity of tumor cell genomes to undergo dynamic changes during evolution
Tumor Suppressor Function: The ability to inhibit tumorigenesis or progression under specific conditions; LINE-1 exhibits this function in myeloid leukemia
Tumor Suppressor Gene Inactivation: In hepatocellular carcinoma, L1 insertion abolishes the expression of the tumor suppressor gene MCC
Tumor Suppressor Gene Loss: Loss of tumor growth inhibition due to gene deletion or inactivation
Tumor-Specific Insertion: Transposon insertion events detected exclusively in tumor cells and distinct from normal tissue
Tumor Mutational Burden: The number of somatic mutations per megabase in the tumor genome, serving as a marker of genomic instability
Embryogenesis Gene Disruption: Disruption of gene functions essential for embryonic development
Impaired Embryonic Development: Overexpression of retrotransposon RNA alters embryonic morphology and compromises pre-implantation development
Embryonic Integration: Transposon integration events occurring primarily during embryonic development rather than in germ cells
Cytosine Methylation: An epigenetic DNA modification involving cytosine methylation that plays a critical role in suppressing LINE-1 transcription
Cytosine Deamination: APOBEC3A-catalyzed deamination of cytidine to uridine in single-stranded DNA substrates (C-to-U)
Cytidine Deamination: APOBEC3-mediated DNA modification process that may restrict LINE-1 retrotransposition
Cytidine Deaminase-Mediated Regulation: Regulation of LINE-1 activity by AID/APOBEC family proteins via deamination of cytidine to uridine in DNA or RNA
Cytoplasmic Localization: Primary localization of LINE-1 ORF1p protein to cytoplasmic RNA granules, such as P-bodies
Insulin Signaling Pathway: Key signal transduction network mediating cellular responses to insulin, regulating metabolism, growth, and survival
Deamination-Independent Inhibition: Mechanism by which APOBEC1 inhibits LINE-1 retrotransposition independent of its cytidine deaminase activity
Deaminase-Dependent Inhibition: Inhibition mechanism wherein APOBEC3 proteins deaminate cytidines to uridines in LINE-1 cDNA via cytosine deaminase activity, leading to cDNA degradation
Deaminase-Independent Inhibition: Transposon suppression mechanism operating independently of catalytic deaminase activity
Adenine Methylation: N6-methyladenosine (m6A), a common post-transcriptional RNA modification involved in regulating RNA metabolism, including retrotransposon RNA
Adenosine Deaminase-Mediated Regulation: Regulation of LINE-1 activity by ADAR enzymes through deamination of adenosine to inosine in double-stranded RNA
Autonomous Retrotransposition: The 'copy-and-paste' mobilization of transposons (e.g., LINE-1) mediated by their encoded enzymes, including reverse transcriptase and endonuclease
Autonomous Cell Growth: The capacity of transformed cells to sustain proliferation independent of continuous exogenous signaling
Autonomous Transcription: LINE-1-driven self-transcription via an internal promoter within its 5' UTR, a critical determinant of its autonomy
Self-Transposition: The genomic mobilization process intrinsic to LINE-1 elements
Natural Genetic Variation: Genetic differences existing between populations or strains, primarily driven by L1 retrotransposition in this context
Pathogenic Mechanism: The process by which transposon activity contributes to disease onset and progression
Pathogenic Mutation: Disease-causing mutations induced by LINE-1 retrotransposition activity within the human genome
Aryl Hydrocarbon Receptor Activation: Ligand-mediated activation (e.g., by benzo[a]pyrene) of the AhR transcription factor, serving as an upstream event for LINE-1 reactivation in this study
Protein-DNA Interaction: Complex binding characteristics between ORF1p and nucleic acids (including DNA), investigable via methods such as single-molecule force spectroscopy
Protein-Protein Interaction: The capability of ORF1p to engage in interactions with other proteins
Protein Sequestration: MxB binds LINE-1 ORF1p and sequesters it within cytoplasmic granules containing G3BP1
Fusion Transcript Expression: Generation of chimeric RNA molecules formed by the junction of transposon and host gene sequences
Phenotypic Variability: Divergent phenotypic traits observed among individuals sharing the same genotype
Epigenetic Modification: Mechanisms regulating gene expression without altering DNA sequence, often susceptible to environmental influences
Altered Epigenetic Modification: Changes in gene expression mediated by chemical modifications, such as histone modification or DNA methylation, without altering the DNA sequence
Epigenetic Dysregulation: Aberrant alterations in epigenetic states, including DNA methylation and histone modification, triggered by or associated with L1 retrotransposition
Epigenetic Derepression: Reduced binding of repressive epigenetic markers (e.g., MeCP2 and H3K9me3) to LINE-1 sequences, resulting in increased expression
Epigenetic Chromatin Condensation: A mechanism that effectively suppresses LINE-1 expression through chromatin modifications, such as histone methylation
Epigenetic Silencing: The process of suppressing gene or transposon expression via mechanisms such as DNA methylation
Epigenetic Activation: Regulation of gene or transposon expression levels without altering the underlying DNA sequence
Epigenetic Regulation: L1 may influence gene expression by recruiting chromatin-modifying protein complexes without altering the DNA sequence
Altered Epigenetic Regulation: Dysregulated epigenetic control of the L1 5'UTR is associated with the pathophysiology of psychiatric disorders
Upregulated Expression: Abnormally elevated transcriptional levels of genes or transposons
Increased Expression: Enhanced abundance of transposon RNA and proteins achieved through sequence optimization, such as reduced adenine content
Aberrant Expression: Significant alterations in transposon mRNA and protein expression levels under specific cell types or conditions
Expression Regulation: Epigenetic control governing L1 and Alu element expression in human embryonic stem cells
Senescence-Like Phenotype: A senescence-mimicking state observed in cells expressing exogenous full-length LINE-1 or SpORF2 mRNA
Senescence-Associated Inflammation: A chronic, low-grade inflammatory state in senescent cells triggered by the derepression of elements such as LINE-1
Senescent Cell Inflammation: An inflammation state exacerbated by LINE-1 activity within senescent cells
L1 Mechanism Recognition: The potential capacity for the 3'-terminal stem-loop structure of mRNA to be recognized and utilized by the LINE-1 retrotransposition machinery
Memory trace consolidation: The process of recording and preserving information regarding the differentiation or activity states of specific neurons associated with memory traces
Induction of DNA damage: Specifically refers to the capacity of the LINE-1 endonuclease domain (ENp) to cleave DNA and induce damage
Induction of cellular senescence: A tumor-suppressive mechanism that arrests cell division
Induced expression: The process by which specific transcription factors or proteins activate the expression of downstream genes or transposons
Acquisition of regulatory elements: The process whereby promoter-inactivated LINE-1 loci acquire regulatory elements from neighboring genes to restore retrotransposition activity
Regulation of gene expression: TET enzymes co-bind with pluripotency factors at enhancer-like transposons to regulate the expression of highly expressed genes in embryonic stem cells
Modulation of immunostimulatory SINE exp: LINE-1 mobility modulates SINE expression, thereby influencing immune responses
Increased glutaminase C production: Glutaminase C is a key enzyme regulating glutamate synthesis; its increased production may lead to elevated glutamate levels
Disruption of negative feedback loop: L1 insertion into the ST18 gene blocks ST18-mediated repression of its enhancer, thereby disrupting a negative feedback loop
Super-enhancer domain: A specific sub-topologically associating domain (sub-TAD) structure enriched with super-enhancers
Transposition: The process by which LINE-1 elements change their genomic location
Activation of transposable elements: The transition of transposable elements from a silent to an active state, potentially leading to mobilization and insertion
Transposon mobilization: The mediation of one transposon's movement (e.g., Alu) by proteins encoded by another transposon (e.g., LINE-1)
Upregulation of transposon expression: An increase in the transcriptional or protein levels of transposons
Transcriptional derepression: The relief of transcriptional repression on transposons, often mediated by inhibiting DNA methyltransferases
Post-transcriptional genome defense mech: A protective mechanism that suppresses transposon activity at the post-transcriptional level
Post-transcriptional repression: Suppression of LINE-1 activity at the post-transcriptional level via recognition, binding, and in vitro cleavage of its structural regions by the Microprocessor complex
Transcription factor binding site: A region within the LINE-1 5' untranslated region where transcription factors bind to regulate transcription
Transcriptional aberration: Alterations in the gene transcription process resulting in the production of abnormal transcripts
Transcriptional level regulation: Significant reduction of L1 transcript levels in cell cycle-arrested cells, thereby limiting retrotransposition events
Transcriptional silencing: Suppression of gene or transposon transcriptional activity via epigenetic modifications, such as H3K9me3
Transcriptional activation: Initiation of transcription for individual genomic L1HS-Ta copies
Transcriptional correlation: Co-variation or association in transcriptional activity between distinct genes or elements within specific tissues or conditions
Transcriptional expression: Transcription of transposon genes into RNA, a prerequisite for retrotransposition
Dysregulation of transcriptional regulat: Aberrant LINE-1 transcriptional control mechanisms in cancer cells, leading to abnormal expression or altered activity
Distribution of transcription start site: Specific locations and patterns of transcription start sites within gene or transposon promoter regions, correlating with transcriptional activity
Retrotransposition: LINE-1 genomic mobility mediated by a 'copy-and-paste' mechanism
Reverse transcription: ORF2p-catalyzed conversion of LINE-1 RNA into cDNA
Reverse transcription primer: The 3'-OH end generated by L1 endonuclease cleavage serves as a primer for reverse transcription
Inhibition of reverse transcription init: Blockade of the initial step in LINE-1 mRNA reverse transcription into cDNA
Retrotransposition-mediated deletion: A mechanism of genomic DNA sequence loss triggered by retrotransposition events, such as Alu element insertion
Retrotransposition complex interaction: Interactions between retrotransposition complex components and potential cellular partners involved in L1 replication
Inhibition of retrotransposon activity: Suppression of retrotransposon activity (e.g., LINE-1), a pathway enriched in the L1-SIRT6 interaction network
Retrotransposition mechanism: The molecular mechanism by which LINE-1 mobilizes via an RNA intermediate in a 'copy-and-paste' manner
Retrotransposition activity: The capacity of retrotransposons to replicate and insert into new genomic loci via an RNA intermediate
Retrotransposition-associated DNA damage: DNA damage induced by LINE-1 retrotransposition activity
Retrotransposition capacity: The ability of certain epigenetically active L1 elements to mobilize within the genome
Retrotransposition-induced mutagenesis: The process of genomic mutation caused by the insertion activity of retrotransposons, such as LINE-1
Reverse transcriptase fidelity: The ability of L1 reverse transcriptase to faithfully replicate its own transcripts, with a base mismatch rate of approximately 1/7000
Reverse transcriptase and endonuclease a: Reverse transcriptase and endonuclease activities provided by the LINE-1 ORF2p protein
Reverse transcriptase inhibitor sensitiv: The characteristic of LINE-1 reverse transcriptase being sensitive to nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors but resistant to non-nucleoside inhibitors
Loss of hematopoietic stem cell function: The decline in self-renewal and differentiation capacity of hematopoietic stem cells caused by factors such as DNA damage
Genetic variation: LINE-1 retrotransposition activity serves as a significant source of novel genetic variations in the human genome
Alteration of genetic network: Transposon insertion events that potentially disrupt interaction networks among genes
Recombination: The process of genetic material exchange or rearrangement, which can lead to genomic structural variations
Recombination mechanism: The process of gene rearrangement resulting from genomic DNA breaks and aberrant repair
Heavy metal exposure affecting transposi: Exposure to heavy metals such as lead may directly alter the structural parameters of ORF1p, thereby influencing the overall retrotransposition frequency of LINE-1
Lead ion binding: The ability of LINE-1 Open Reading Frame 1 protein (ORF1p) to bind multiple lead ions, utilizing its tri-cysteine layer as a metal-binding site
Mismatch repair: A repair pathway that corrects mismatched bases during DNA replication, with activation linked to transposon insertion activity
Mismatch extension: The capacity of LINE-1 ORF2p reverse transcriptase to extend a DNA primer even when a base mismatch exists at its 3' end
Mesenchymal-to-Epithelial Transition: A critical cell differentiation event during kidney development that can be disrupted by L1
Indirect Replication of Non-autonomous R: LINE-1 facilitates the amplification of non-autonomous retrotransposons (e.g., SINEs) in the genome via its reverse transcription machinery
Restriction of Retrotransposition: The suppression of retrotransposon mobility, such as LINE-1, by host factors like APOBEC3B
Random Insertional Mutagenesis: Utilizing the retrotransposition activity of L1 elements as vectors to randomly insert into the genome and induce mutations
Sequestration of Tumor Suppressors: The oncogenic mechanism whereby LINE-1-encoded proteins ORF1p and ORF2p bind to and sequester tumor suppressors
Retrotransposition of Non-L1 Transcripts: Following displacement from its RNA template during TPRT, ORF1p may bind and disrupt residual L1 RNPs, rendering them susceptible to hijacking by non-L1 templates to mediate retrotransposition
Non-LTR Retrotransposition: The genomic mobilization of retrotransposons like LINE-1 via a 'copy-and-paste' mechanism
Production of Non-functional ORF1p Varia: Functionally impaired ORF1 proteins encoded by spliced retrotransposable elements integrated within the 5'UTR or ORF1 regions
Non-reference L1 Polymorphic Insertions: L1 insertions present or absent among individuals that are not annotated in the current reference genome assembly
Non-homologous End Joining: A DNA double-strand break repair pathway
Unconventional Translation Mechanisms: A protein translation mechanism that operates independently of the canonical AUG start codon
Non-insertional Mechanisms: Mechanisms by which L1 elements exert effects without genomic insertion, such as modulating gene expression networks
Non-allelic Homologous Recombination: An aberrant recombination process mediated by flanking repetitive sequences (e.g., segmental duplications or retrotransposons) that can lead to genomic rearrangements
Non-coding RNA Regulation: Regulatory functions exerted by L1 RNA at the non-coding level, influencing cellular mitotic potential
Target Site Cleavage: Cleavage of genomic DNA by L1 endonuclease to generate target sites for L1 insertion
Target Site Duplication: Short DNA sequence repeats flanking an insertion site generated upon L1 integration, serving as a hallmark of endonuclease-dependent integration
Target-primed Reverse Transcription: A LINE-1 transposition mechanism wherein endonuclease-mediated cleavage of genomic DNA releases a 3'-hydroxyl group that primes reverse transcription of LINE-1 RNA by LINE-1 reverse transcriptase
Target Site Selection: The process by which LINE-1 transposons recognize and cleave specific genomic sequences via their endonuclease domain to determine insertion loci
Targeted DNA Cleavage: A reaction catalyzed by NLR-encoded proteins resulting in the cleavage of specific target DNA strands
Target-primed Retrotransposition: A retrotransposition mechanism wherein transposons mobilize by reverse transcribing their RNA and integrating the resulting DNA copy into a new genomic locus
Targeted Insertion: Guiding LINE-1 ORF2p via fused DNA-binding domains (DBDs) to direct retrotransposition events to specific genomic loci
Target Genomic DNA Deletion: Loss of target genomic DNA fragments associated with endonuclease-independent LINE-1 insertion
Alteration of Target Gene Expression: Modulation of gene expression near the integration site following LINE-1 retrotransposon insertion
Cis/Trans-assisted Reverse Transcription: LINE-1 facilitates reverse transcription and genomic integration of other RNAs, such as mRNAs and small RNAs, in addition to self-replication
Cis-acting: Provision of essential molecular machinery by LINE-1 for the retrotransposition of other mobile elements, such as Alu
Cis-preference: The tendency of LINE-1 to preferentially retrotranspose its own concurrently translated transcripts
Cis-preferential Reverse Transcription: The propensity of LINE-1-encoded reverse transcriptase (ORF2p) to prioritize its own encoded RNA as a template for reverse transcription
Cis-mobilization: Preferential or exclusive mobilization of LINE-1-encoded RNA by its own protein machinery
Cis-activity: The capacity of LINE-1-encoded proteins to mediate their own retrotransposition
Provision of Cis-regulatory Elements: Host co-option of LINE-1 sequences to supply regulatory elements for gene expression
Cis-transposition: LINE-1 reverse transcribes its own RNA copies and inserts them into new genomic locations
Cis-retrotransposition: LINE-1 mediates the retrotransposition of its own sequences
Extra Nucleotide Insertion: Non-templated additional nucleotide sequences occurring at LINE-1 insertion sites
Driving Alu and SVA Transposition: The LINE-1 retrotransposition machinery drives the cis-mobilization of non-autonomous Alu and SVA transposons
Driver Mutation: Mutations that promote tumorigenesis, progression, and metastasis
High Processivity: LINE-1 reverse transcriptase exhibits higher processivity in vitro compared to Moloney murine leukemia virus reverse transcriptase
Efficient Delivery: The effective introduction of genetic material into target cells
High-throughput Dual-luciferase Assay: A high-sensitivity, wide-dynamic-range functional assay using firefly luciferase as a retrotransposition reporter and Renilla luciferase for normalization
